feedback.text: I bought the game "The Crew", even paying extra for a "special edition", and then it was pulled from my games account before I had a chance to play it, so I basically paid money for a product that was taken from me before I enjoyed it, because the game I intended to enjoy as an individual experience included online functions that stopped being supported. This was a decision taken by the game publisher at their own discretion following their own opaque, internal criteria. This has happened regularly and it's not going to stop. At this point in history, game developers have total freedom to make any game with any online features unplayable whenever they want, with no restrictions at all, instead of taking any course of action to keep this games in any kind of functional state. This is completely unfair, violating my customer rights. There is a pattern of the games industry doing that, so self-regulation is not going to work, and the same protections applied to any other product should apply here. There's no reason this multi-million industry should be exempted from offering basic customer rights. There is zero regulation about this, so at this point the industry dictates all the rules and some of these practices are clearly unfair. Also, predatory practices like gambling mechanisms and microtransactions should be taken seriously. Sometimes being a video game player feels like living in the "Far West". Regulation is urgently needed to protect players.
